--- SOURCE 2: Mapping epigenetic modifications by sequencing ... ---
URL: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41418-023-01213-1

SUMMARY:
In this review, we summarize the diversity and complexity of epigenetic modifications and their related sequencing methods, focusing on recent advances in quantitative and base-resolution technologies for major DNA (5mC/5hmC/5fC/5caC) and RNA (m6A/Ψ) modifications in mammalian cells. A comprehensive overview of the progression of next-generation sequencing techniques developed over time for detecting major Histone, DNA (5mC, 5hmC, 5fC, 5caC) and RNA (m6A, Ψ, m1A, m5C/ hm5C, m7G, m3C) modifications. Their efficiencies and accuracies are still lacking compared to DNA methylation sequencing methods, highlighted by the fact that different methods for the same modification often produce very distinct profiles (e.g., m6A and Ψ maps of the same cell line obtained by different sequencing methods showed low overlaps) [8A methylation in the mammalian transcriptome using GLORI.

# Mapping epigenetic modifications by sequencing technologies

[*Cell Death & Differentiation*](/cdd)
**volume 32**, pages 56–65 (2025)[Cite this article](#citeas)

22k Accesses

21 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

The “epigenetics” concept was first described in 1942. Thus far, chemical modifications on histones, DNA, and RNA have emerged as three important building blocks of epigenetic modifications. Many epigenetic modifications have been intensively studied and found to be involved in most essential biological processes as well as human diseases, including cancer. Precisely and quantitatively mapping over 100 [[1](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR1 "Zhao Y, Garcia BA. Comprehensive catalog of currently documented histone modifications. Cold Spring Harb Perspect Biol. 2015;7:a025064.")], 17 [[2](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR2 "Gommers-Ampt JH, Borst P. Hypermodified bases in DNA. FASEB J. 1995;9:1034–42.")], and 160 [[3](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR3 "Boccaletto P, Machnicka MA, Purta E, Piatkowski P, Baginski B, Wirecki TK, et al. MODOMICS: a database of RNA modification pathways. 2017 update. Nucleic Acids Res. 2018;46:D303–D7.")] different known types of epigenetic modifications in histone, DNA, and RNA is the key to understanding the role of epigenetic modifications in gene regulation in diverse biological processes. With the rapid development of sequencing technologies, scientists are able to detect specific epigenetic modifications with various quantitative, high-resolution, whole-genome/transcriptome approaches. Here, we summarize recent advances in epigenetic modification sequencing technologies, focusing on major histone, DNA, and RNA modifications in mammalian cells.

![](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41418-023-01213-1/MediaObjects/41418_2023_1213_Figa_HTML.png)

### [Computational analysis of DNA methylation from long-read sequencing](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41576-025-00822-5?fromPaywallRec=false)

## Facts

Traditional, bisulfite sequencing is the gold standard in DNA methylation sequencing but it severely damages DNA [[4](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR4 "Tanaka K, Okamoto A. Degradation of DNA by bisulfite treatment. Bioorg Med Chem Lett. 2007;17:1912–5.")].

Recently, EM-Seq and TAPS have been developed to replace bisulfite sequencing [[5](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR5 "Liu Y, Siejka-Zielinska P, Velikova G, Bi Y, Yuan F, Tomkova M, et al. Bisulfite-free direct detection of 5-methylcytosine and 5-hydroxymethylcytosine at base resolution. Nat Biotechnol. 2019;37:424–9."), [6](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR6 "Vaisvila R, Ponnaluri VKC, Sun Z, Langhorst BW, Saleh L, Guan S, et al. Enzymatic methyl sequencing detects DNA methylation at single-base resolution from picograms of DNA. Genome Res. 2021;31:1280–9.")].

Very recently, base-resolution and quantitative RNA epitranscriptomic modifications sequencing methods have started to emerge [[7](#ref-CR7 "Hu L, Liu S, Peng Y, Ge R, Su R, Senevirathne C, et al. m(6)A RNA modifications are measured at single-base resolution across the mammalian transcriptome. Nat Biotechnol. 2022;40:1210–9."),[8](#ref-CR8 "Liu C, Sun H, Yi Y, Shen W, Li K, Xiao Y, et al. Absolute quantification of single-base m(6)A methylation in the mammalian transcriptome using GLORI. Nat Biotechnol. 2023;41:355–66."),[9](#ref-CR9 "Xiao Y-L, Liu S, Ge R, Wu Y, He C, Chen M, et al. Transcriptome-wide profiling and quantification of N6-methyladenosine by enzyme-assisted adenosine deamination. Nat Biotechnol. 2023;41:993–1003."),[10](#ref-CR10 "Dai Q, Zhang LS, Sun HL, Pajdzik K, Yang L, Ye C, et al. Quantitative sequencing using BID-seq uncovers abundant pseudouridines in mammalian mRNA at base resolution. Nat Biotechnol. 2023;41:344–54."),[11](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR11 "Zhang M, Jiang Z, Ma Y, Liu W, Zhuang Y, Lu B, et al. Quantitative profiling of pseudouridylation landscape in the human transcriptome. Nat Chem Biol. 2023.")].

Third-generation sequencing is promising for epigenetic sequencing; however, many challenges remain to be resolved [[12](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR12 "Lucas MC, Novoa EM. Long-read sequencing in the era of epigenomics and epitranscriptomics. Nat Methods. 2023;20:25–9.")].

## Open questions

Could further RNA modification sequencing methods be developed with improved efficiency and accuracy?

Could simultaneous base-resolution sequencing of a wide range of epigenetic modifications be achieved?

Could third-generation sequencing finally deliver epigenetic data as accurate and cost-effective as next-generation sequencing?

Could we develop large-scale live cell temporal/spatial epigenetic sequencing?

## Introduction

In 1942, embryologist Conrad Waddington first established the concept of “epigenetics” with the famous “epigenetic landscape” model [[13](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR13 "Deichmann U. Epigenetics: the origins and evolution of a fashionable topic. Dev Biol. 2016;416:249–54.")]. However, the explosion of epigenetic studies has only occurred over the last two decades. The term “epigenetic” refers to the alteration of gene expression without change of the DNA sequence, which mainly occurs in the form of a myriad of chemical modifications in histone, DNA, and RNA. Among them, chromatin structure modification studies started in the 1990s. Over 100 distinct modifications have been found in histone, including acetylation (Ac), methylation (Me), phosphorylation (P), ubiquitylation (Ub), SUMOylation (SUMO), ADP ribosylation (ADP), O-GlcNAcylation (O-Glc), and biotinylation (Biotin) (Fig. [1](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#Fig1)) [[1](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR1 "Zhao Y, Garcia BA. Comprehensive catalog of currently documented histone modifications. Cold Spring Harb Perspect Biol. 2015;7:a025064."), [14](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR14 "Kouzarides T. Chromatin modifications and their function. Cell. 2007;128:693–705."), [15](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR15 "Zentner GE, Henikoff S. Regulation of nucleosome dynamics by histone modifications. Nat Struct Mol Biol. 2013;20:259–66.")]. In the late 1940s, 5-methylcytosine (5mC) was the first identified DNA chemical modification [[16](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR16 "Moore LD, Le T, Fan G. DNA methylation and its basic function. Neuropsychopharmacology. 2013;38:23–38.")], and to date, over 17 types of DNA chemical modifications have been identified [[2](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR2 "Gommers-Ampt JH, Borst P. Hypermodified bases in DNA. FASEB J. 1995;9:1034–42.")]. 5mC is the most predominant and important modification in mammalian DNA, the so-called “fifth base”. Its oxidative products, 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC), 5-formylcytosine (5fC), and 5-carboxylcytosine (5caC), also exist in mammalian DNA (Fig. [1](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#Fig1)). Compared to DNA, RNA modifications are more diverse, with over 160 types of RNA modifications reported thus far [[3](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR3 "Boccaletto P, Machnicka MA, Purta E, Piatkowski P, Baginski B, Wirecki TK, et al. MODOMICS: a database of RNA modification pathways. 2017 update. Nucleic Acids Res. 2018;46:D303–D7.")]. The most common dynamic RNA modifications include *N*6-methyladenosine (m6A), pseudouridine (Ψ), *N*1-methyladenosine (m1A), *N*7-methylguanosine (m7G), 5-methylcytidine (m5C) / 5-hydroxymethylcytidine (hm5C) in mammalian cells, leading to the exciting field of epitranscriptomics [[17](#ref-CR17 "Arzumanian VA, Dolgalev GV, Kurbatov IY, Kiseleva OI, Poverennaya EV Epitranscriptome: review of top 25 most-studied RNA modifications. Int J Mol Sci. 2022;23:13851."),[18](#ref-CR18 "Barbieri I, Kouzarides T. Role of RNA modifications in cancer. Nat Rev Cancer. 2020;20:303–22."),[19](#ref-CR19 "Zhao LY, Song J, Liu Y, Song CX, Yi C. Mapping the epigenetic modifications of DNA and RNA. Protein Cell. 2020;11:792–808."),[20](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR20 "Fu Y, He C. Nucleic acid modifications with epigenetic significance. Curr Opin Chem Biol. 2012;16:516–24.")] (Fig. [1](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#Fig1)). In the past two decades, the dysregulation of epigenetic modifications has been revealed in many studies and serves as a hallmark of cancer: many somatic mutations in human cancers occur in epigenetic regulators [[21](#ref-CR21 "Consortium ITP-CAoWG. Pan-cancer analysis of whole genomes. Nature. 2020;578:82–93."),[22](#ref-CR22 "Ley TJ, Ding L, Walter MJ, McLellan MD, Lamprecht T, Larson DE, et al. DNMT3A mutations in acute myeloid leukemia. N Engl J Med. 2010;363:2424–33."),[23](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR23 "Cancer Genome Atlas Research N, Ley TJ, Miller C, Ding L, Raphael BJ, Mungall AJ, et al. Genomic and epigenomic landscapes of adult de novo acute myeloid leukemia. N Engl J Med. 2013;368:2059–74.")].

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41418-023-01213-1/MediaObjects/41418_2023_1213_Fig1_HTML.png)

Histone Modifications: Nucleosomes are composed of DNA wrapped around the four core histones (H3, H4, H2A, H2B). Post-translational modifications, including acetylation (Ac), methylation (Me), phosphorylation (P), ubiquitylation (Ub), SUMOylation (SUMO), ADP ribosylation (ADP), O-GlcNAcylation (O-Glc), and biotinylation (Biotin), are commonly observed on the N-terminal histone tails. Notably, significant modifications of Histone H3 have been associated with either active or repressive gene expression. Major DNA Modifications in Mammals: DNA modifications include DNA 5-methylcytosine (5mC), 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC), 5-formylcytosine (5fC), and 5-carboxylcytosine (5caC). These modifications possess distinct chemical structures and are crucial in epigenetic regulation. Major RNA modifications in Mammals: RNA modifications include *N*6-methyladenosine (m6A), pseudouridine (Ψ), *N*1-methyladenosine (m1A), *N*7-methylguanosine (m7G), 5-methylcytidine (m5C) and 5-hydroxymethylcytidine (hm5C) modifications, and their chemical structures.

The first wave of methods to identify these epigenetic modifications used bulk measurements such as thin layer chromatography (TLC), LC/GC-MS, immunofluorescence, and immunoprecipitation. These methods, however, could not provide sequence information about the modifications [[24](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR24 "Dai Y, Yuan BF, Feng YQ. Quantification and mapping of DNA modifications. RSC Chem Biol. 2021;2:1096–114.")]. With the rapid development of next generation sequencing (NGS), sequencing based detection methods for epigenetic modifications have been developed at a rapid pace. Earlier methods usually relied on affinity enrichment (i.e. immunoprecipitation, biotin pull-down, etc.). While useful and cost-effective, they only provide limited semi-quantitative and low-resolution (a few hundred base pairs) information about the modification. More recent developments focus on high-resolution (e.g., base-level resolution) and quantitative sequencing methods, which provide a more complete picture of the modification. While NGS has significantly advanced the field, it still has limitations, including short read lengths, biases introduced by amplification steps, and difficulties in accurately resolving repetitive genomic regions. In this regard, the future development of third-generation sequencing emerges as a promising solution [[25](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR25 "Rhoads A, Au KF. PacBio sequencing and its applications. Genom Proteom Bioinforma. 2015;13:278–89."), [26](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR26 "Searle B, Muller M, Carell T, Kellett A. Third-generation sequencing of epigenetic DNA. Angew Chem Int Ed Engl. 2022;62:e202215704.")]. In this review, we summarize the diversity and complexity of epigenetic modifications and their related sequencing methods, focusing on recent advances in quantitative and base-resolution technologies for major DNA (5mC/5hmC/5fC/5caC) and RNA (m6A/Ψ) modifications in mammalian cells. This review is an update and extension of our previous summary on DNA and RNA modification detection [[19](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR19 "Zhao LY, Song J, Liu Y, Song CX, Yi C. Mapping the epigenetic modifications of DNA and RNA. Protein Cell. 2020;11:792–808.")].

## Histone modification detection by sequencing technologies

Chromatin is composed of DNA and histone proteins with nucleosomes as the basic structural units. Around 147 base pairs of DNA are packaged into an octamer of the four core histones (H3, H4, H2A, H2B) as the nucleosome [[27](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR27 "Ruiz-Carrillo A, Jorcano JL. An octamer of core histones in solution: central role of the H3-H4 tetramer in the self-assembly. Biochemistry. 1979;18:760–8.")]. Histone modifications usually occur at the unstructured N-terminal of the histones, which could modify chromatin accessibility to chromatin remodeling enzymes and transcription factors, thus regulating gene expression [[14](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR14 "Kouzarides T. Chromatin modifications and their function. Cell. 2007;128:693–705."), [28](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR28 "Bartova E, Krejci J, Harnicarova A, Galiova G, Kozubek S. Histone modifications and nuclear architecture: a review. J Histochem Cytochem. 2008;56:711–21.")]. Some modifications such as H3K27ac and H3K4me1/2/3 are active markers, while others such as H3K27me3 and H3K9me3 are repressive markers in histone H3 tail (Fig. [1](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#Fig1)) [[14](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR14 "Kouzarides T. Chromatin modifications and their function. Cell. 2007;128:693–705."), [29](#ref-CR29 "Zhou VW, Goren A, Bernstein BE. Charting histone modifications and the functional organization of mammalian genomes. Nat Rev Genet. 2011;12:7–18."),[30](#ref-CR30 "Zhao Z, Shilatifard A. Epigenetic modifications of histones in cancer. Genome Biol. 2019;20:245."),[31](#ref-CR31 "Rosenfeld JA, Wang Z, Schones DE, Zhao K, DeSalle R, Zhang MQ. Determination of enriched histone modifications in non-genic portions of the human genome. BMC Genom. 2009;10:143."),[32](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR32 "Messier TL, Gordon JA, Boyd JR, Tye CE, Browne G, Stein JL, et al. Histone H3 lysine 4 acetylation and methylation dynamics define breast cancer subtypes. Oncotarget. 2016;7:5094–109.")]. It has been widely established that dysregulation of histone modifications causes a number of diseases, including cancer [[30](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR30 "Zhao Z, Shilatifard A. Epigenetic modifications of histones in cancer. Genome Biol. 2019;20:245."), [33](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR33 "Esteller M. Cancer epigenomics: DNA methylomes and histone-modification maps. Nat Rev Genet. 2007;8:286–98."), [34](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR34 "Portela A, Esteller M. Epigenetic modifications and human disease. Nat Biotechnol. 2010;28:1057–68.")]. It is therefore important to study their location and distribution across the genome. Among many approaches to mapping these modifications, chromatin immunoprecipitation followed by sequencing (ChIP–Seq) is the most classical method to sequence histone modifications in a genome-wide manner. ChIP–Seq is based on formaldehyde/paraformaldehyde-mediated protein-DNA crosslinking, followed by incubation with the specific antibodies to enrich the target histone modification and finally library construction for next-generation sequencing to detect genome-wide histone modification distribution. The earliest formaldehyde-mediated protein-DNA crosslinking ChIP experiment was conducted by Solomon et al. [[35](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR35 "Solomon MJ, Larsen PL, Varshavsky A. Mapping protein-DNA interactions in vivo with formaldehyde: evidence that histone H4 is retained on a highly transcribed gene. Cell. 1988;53:937–47.")] to probe histone H4 and hsp70 DNA interactions in vivo in 1988, whilst the first ChIP-Seq method was established by Barski et al. in 2007, who mapped genome-wide distributions of 20 histone lysine and arginine methylations [[36](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR36 "Barski A, Cuddapah S, Cui K, Roh TY, Schones DE, Wang Z, et al. High-resolution profiling of histone methylations in the human genome. Cell. 2007;129:823–37.")]. Despite this technique being widely used, ChIP–Seq has several limitations, such as the requirement for larger amounts of input DNA, false positive rates induced by cross-linking between DNA and protein, and high background noise due to poor antibody specificity. [[37](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR37 "Park PJ. ChIP-seq: advantages and challenges of a maturing technology. Nat Rev Genet. 2009;10:669–80.")]. Recently, two novel technologies have been established to overcome these limitations. In 2017, Cleavage Under Targets and Release Using Nuclease (CUT&RUN) technology was described, for semi-quantitative detection of protein-DNA interactions in situ at ~20 bp resolution [[38](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR38 "Skene PJ, Henikoff S. An efficient targeted nuclease strategy for high-resolution mapping of DNA binding sites. Elife. 2017;6:e21856."), [39](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR39 "Meers MP, Bryson TD, Henikoff JG, Henikoff S. Improved CUT&RUN chromatin profiling tools. Elife. 2019;8:e46314.")] (Fig. [2](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#Fig2)). To avoid the epitope masking and false positive binding sites generated by the crosslinking step in ChIP-Seq, CUT&RUN immobilizes the cells on lectin-coated magnetic beads and incubates with specific antibodies and protein A-MNase. Ca2+ was then added to initiate the cleavage reaction to release the target protein-DNA complexes for sequencing. In 2019, an improved Cleavage Under Targets and Tagmentation (CUT&Tag) technology was developed, which replaces the MNase digestion with Tn5 tagmentation (Fig. [2](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#Fig2)). CUT&Tag simplifies the library construction steps [[40](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR40 "Kaya-Okur HS, Wu SJ, Codomo CA, Pledger ES, Bryson TD, Henikoff JG, et al. CUT&Tag for efficient epigenomic profiling of small samples and single cells. Nat Commun. 2019;10:1930.")], making it more feasible for single-cell experiments to probe histone modifications, such as those that are characteristic of active promoters (H3K4me3), enhancers (H3K27ac), gene bodies (H3K36me3) and inactive regions (H3K27me3), as demonstrated in mice brains [[41](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR41 "Bartosovic M, Kabbe M, Castelo-Branco G. Single-cell CUT&Tag profiles histone modifications and transcription factors in complex tissues. Nat Biotechnol. 2021;39:825–35.")]. However, CUT&RUN and CUT&Tag inherit the disadvantages of antibody-based approaches. Future development could focus on novel antibody-free and enrichment-free approaches to map histone modifications quantitatively at single base resolution.

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41418-023-01213-1/MediaObjects/41418_2023_1213_Fig2_HTML.png)

A comprehensive overview of the progression of next-generation sequencing techniques developed over time for detecting major Histone, DNA (5mC, 5hmC, 5fC, 5caC) and RNA (m6A, Ψ, m1A, m5C/ hm5C, m7G, m3C) modifications.

## DNA methylation (5mC) sequencing technologies

DNA methylation of cytosine (5mC) is the most predominant modification, and accounts for ~5% of all cytosine (C) [[42](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR42 "Song CX, He C. Potential functional roles of DNA demethylation intermediates. Trends Biochem Sci. 2013;38:480–4.")]. 5mC modification on DNA is catalyzed by a family of enzymes known as DNA methyl transferases (DNMTs), predominantly in the symmetrical CpG dinucleotides: 70–80% of CpGs in the mammalian genome are methylated [[43](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR43 "Bird A. DNA methylation patterns and epigenetic memory. Genes Dev. 2002;16:6–21.")]. DNA methylation has been found to relate to X chromosome inactivation, and genomic imprinting, and is dysregulated in most diseases, including cancer [[33](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR33 "Esteller M. Cancer epigenomics: DNA methylomes and histone-modification maps. Nat Rev Genet. 2007;8:286–98."), [34](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR34 "Portela A, Esteller M. Epigenetic modifications and human disease. Nat Biotechnol. 2010;28:1057–68."), [44](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR44 "Reik W, Dean W, Walter J. Epigenetic reprogramming in mammalian development. Science. 2001;293:1089–93.")]. The hypermethylation of tumor suppressor genes or hypomethylation of tumor oncogenes has been commonly identified in many cancers [[45](#ref-CR45 "Esteller M. CpG island hypermethylation and tumor suppressor genes: a booming present, a brighter future. Oncogene. 2002;21:5427–40."),[46](#ref-CR46 "Feinberg AP, Vogelstein B. Hypomethylation of ras oncogenes in primary human cancers. Biochem Biophys Res Commun. 1983;111:47–54."),[47](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR47 "Van Tongelen A, Loriot A, De Smet C. Oncogenic roles of DNA hypomethylation through the activation of cancer-germline genes. Cancer Lett. 2017;396:130–7.")]. This makes DNA methylation an attractive therapeutic target for cancer treatment. For example, DNA methylation inhibitors, such as 5-azacytidine, 5-aza-2’-deoxycytidine (decitabine, DAC), and cytarabine (Ara-C), have been widely used to inhibit tumor growth in vivo and also applied to treat myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) and acute myeloid leukemia (AML) patients [[48](#ref-CR48 "Roulois D, Loo Yau H, Singhania R, Wang Y, Danesh A, Shen SY, et al. DNA-demethylating agents target colorectal cancer cells by inducing viral mimicry by endogenous transcripts. Cell. 2015;162:961–73."),[49](#ref-CR49 "Blagitko-Dorfs N, Schlosser P, Greve G, Pfeifer D, Meier R, Baude A, et al. Combination treatment of acute myeloid leukemia cells with DNMT and HDAC inhibitors: predominant synergistic gene downregulation associated with gene body demethylation. Leukemia. 2019;33:945–56."),[50](#ref-CR50 "Kelly TK, De Carvalho DD, Jones PA. Epigenetic modifications as therapeutic targets. Nat Biotechnol. 2010;28:1069–78."),[51](#ref-CR51 "Salehi B, Selamoglu Z, S Mileski K, Pezzani R, Redaelli M, Cho WC, et al. Liposomal cytarabine as cancer therapy: from chemistry to medicine. Biomolecules. 2019;9:773."),[52](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR52 "Chen X, Zhou W, Song RH, Liu S, Wang S, Chen Y, et al. Tumor suppressor CEBPA interacts with and inhibits DNMT3A activity. Sci Adv. 2022;8:eabl5220.")].

In 2005, MeDIP-Seq (Fig. [2](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#Fig2)) was developed to capture differential DNA methylation in normal and transformed human cells [[53](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR53 "Weber M, Davies JJ, Wittig D, Oakeley EJ, Haase M, Lam WL, et al. Chromosome-wide and promoter-specific analyses identify sites of differential DNA methylation in normal and transformed human cells. Nat Genet. 2005;37:853–62.")]. However, as it is based on antibody enrichment, it suffers from the same limitations as ChIP-Seq. In 1992, bisulfite treatment for 5mC detection was first developed by Frommer, et al. [[54](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR54 "Frommer M, McDonald LE, Millar DS, Collis CM, Watt F, Grigg GW, et al. A genomic sequencing protocol that yields a positive display of 5-methylcytosine residues in individual DNA strands. Proc Natl Acad Sci USA. 1992;89:1827–31.")]. This method utilizes sodium bisulfite, which specifically deaminates unmodified C to Uracil (U) while leaving 5mC and 5hmC intact. During the subsequent PCR and sequencing, U is therefore read as thymine (T). When comparing bisulfite-converted sequences with the reference genome, 5mC and 5hmC can be distinguished from unmodified C. Based on this bisulfite reaction, the base-resolution and quantitative whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) (Fig. [2](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#Fig2)) for 5mC and 5hmC mapping was developed in 2009 [[55](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR55 "Lister R, Pelizzola M, Dowen RH, Hawkins RD, Hon G, Tonti-Filippini J, et al. Human DNA methylomes at base resolution show widespread epigenomic differences. Nature. 2009;462:315-22.")]. With over 99% conversion of unmodified C to U, WGBS became the powerful and widely used gold standard for mapping 5mC and 5hmC (Fig. [3](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#Fig3)). However, WGBS has two significant drawbacks. First, bisulfite treatment is a very harsh chemical reaction that will cause severe DNA damage and loss [[4](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR4 "Tanaka K, Okamoto A. Degradation of DNA by bisulfite treatment. Bioorg Med Chem Lett. 2007;17:1912–5.")]. Second, the converted unmodified cytosine, which accounts for around 95% of all cytosine in the genome, leads to reduced DNA sequence complexity, lower mapping efficiency, and biased genomic coverage after bisulfite treatment. Nevertheless, various improvements have been made, such as post-bisulfite adaptor tagging (PBAT), which salvages fragmented DNA caused by bisulfite treatment to mitigate the bisulfite-induced loss of intact sequencing templates [[56](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR56 "Miura F, Enomoto Y, Dairiki R, Ito T. Amplification-free whole-genome bisulfite sequencing by post-bisulfite adaptor tagging. Nucleic Acids Res. 2012;40:e136.")]. By adopting PBAT, various single-cell WGBS protocols have been developed [[57](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR57 "Clark SJ, Smallwood SA, Lee HJ, Krueger F, Reik W, Kelsey G. Genome-wide base-resolution mapping of DNA methylation in single cells using single-cell bisulfite sequencing (scBS-seq). Nat Protoc. 2017;12:534–47."), [58](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR58 "Farlik M, Sheffield NC, Nuzzo A, Datlinger P, Schonegger A, Klughammer J, et al. Single-cell DNA methylome sequencing and bioinformatic inference of epigenomic cell-state dynamics. Cell Rep. 2015;10:1386–97.")].

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41418-023-01213-1/MediaObjects/41418_2023_1213_Fig3_HTML.png)

Summary of prominent techniques employed in the detection of DNA 5mC and 5hmC epigenetic modifications. Bisulfite-based and indirect methodologies encompass bisulfite sequencing (BS-Seq) for both 5mC and 5hmC, oxidative bisulfite sequencing (oxBS-Seq) for 5mC, and TET-assisted bisulfite sequencing (TAB-Seq) for 5hmC. Bisulfite-free yet indirect methods include enzymatic methyl-Seq (EM-Seq) for both 5mC and 5hmC and APOBEC-coupled epigenetic sequencing (ACE-Seq) for 5hmC. Alternatively, bisulfite-free and direct strategies entail chemical-assisted C-to-T conversion of 5hmC sequencing (hmC-CATCH) for 5hmC, TET-assisted pyridine borane sequencing (TAPS) for both 5mC and 5hmC, TAPSβ for 5mC, chemical-assisted pyridine borane sequencing plus (CAPS+) for 5hmC.

To overcome the limitations of WGBS, two bisulfite-free DNA methylation sequencing methods have recently been developed. The enzymatic methyl-Seq (EM-Seq) (Fig. [2](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#Fig2)) is an enzymatic deamination method involving three enzymes in two reactions [[6](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR6 "Vaisvila R, Ponnaluri VKC, Sun Z, Langhorst BW, Saleh L, Guan S, et al. Enzymatic methyl sequencing detects DNA methylation at single-base resolution from picograms of DNA. Genome Res. 2021;31:1280–9.")]. In the first reaction, Tet methylcytosine dioxygenase 2 (TET2) is used to catalyze the oxidization of 5mC to 5hmC, 5fC, and 5caC. In the same reaction, β-glucosyltransferase (βGT) glucosylates both TET2-derived and genomic 5hmC to form 5-(β-glucosyloxymethyl) cytosine (5gmC). In the second reaction, AID/APOBEC family DNA deaminase APOBEC3A deaminates unmodified C to U, while 5fC, 5caC, and 5gmC are protected from APOBEC3A deamination (Fig. [3](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#Fig3)). EM-Seq achieved over 96% protection rate on 5mC with less than 0.6% non-conversion rate (false positive rate) of unmodified cytosine. The mild enzymatic reactions make EM-Seq compatible with input DNA quantities as low as 100 pg. Recently, single-cell EM-Seq has also been established [[59](/articles/s41418-023-01213-1#ref-CR59 "Wang J, Fang Y, Chen W, Zhang C, Chen Z, Xie Z, et al. High coverage of single cell genomes by T7-assisted enzymatic methyl-sequencing. bioRxiv. 2022:2022.02.2

Epigenetics Market: 10 Emerging Trends Redefining Research & Therapeutics
=========================================================================

[](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://extrapolate.com/blog/top-10-trends-epigenetics-market-2025&quote=Epigenetics%20Market:%2010%20Emerging%20Trends%20Redefining%20Research%20&%20Therapeutics)[](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=Epigenetics%20Market:%2010%20Emerging%20Trends%20Redefining%20Research%20&%20Therapeutics&url=https://extrapolate.com/blog/top-10-trends-epigenetics-market-2025)[](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https://extrapolate.com/blog/top-10-trends-epigenetics-market-2025)[](https://mail.google.com/mail/u/0/?view=cm&su=Epigenetics%20Market:%2010%20Emerging%20Trends%20Redefining%20Research%20&%20Therapeutics&body=https://extrapolate.com/blog/top-10-trends-epigenetics-market-2025)

[Patricia](https://www.extrapolate.com/author/patricia)  05-03-2025 

The epigenetics market is witnessing a wave of advancements, revolutionizing how scientists understand gene regulation and disease mechanisms. Epigenetic modifications influence various biological processes, playing a crucial role in personalized medicine, drug development, and even agricultural improvements. As research accelerates, breakthroughs in epigenetic biomarkers, AI-driven data analysis, and environmental epigenetics are opening new frontiers in medicine and biotechnology. According to Extrapolate, the [global epigenetics market](https://www.extrapolate.com/healthcare-medical-devices-biotechnology/epigenetics-market/87495) will reach USD 3.34 billion in 2032 due to rising research investments and expanding clinical applications.

**The Top 10 Trends Driving Innovation in the Epigenetics Market and Shaping Its Future:**

**1. Rising Focus on Personalized Medicine**

Epigenetics is unlocking new possibilities in tailored treatments, allowing medical professionals to customize therapies based on an individual’s unique epigenetic profile. Targeting epigenetic modifications in diseases such as cancer, autoimmune disorders, and neurodegenerative conditions enhances treatment efficacy while minimizing side effects. The push toward precision medicine is making epigenetic therapies a crucial component of modern healthcare.

In November 2024, Epigenic Therapeutics received approval from New Zealand's Medsafe and the Health and Disability Ethics Committees (HDEC) to commence clinical trials for EPI-003, an innovative liver-targeting antiviral therapy for chronic hepatitis B (CHB). EPI-003 utilizes Epigenic's proprietary EPIREG platform, combining mRNA encoding an epigenetic modulation protein with a guide RNA targeting HBV-specific genes, all encapsulated within lipid nanoparticles.

**2. Expansion of Epigenetic Drug Development**

Pharmaceutical companies are investing heavily in epigenetic drug development, particularly for cancer and neurological disorders. Histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitors and DNA methylation inhibitors are gaining prominence as promising therapeutic agents. As the epigenetics market expands, new drugs targeting specific epigenetic pathways are expected to enter clinical trials, offering hope for improved disease management.

**3. Advancements in Epigenetic Editing**

CRISPR-based epigenetic editing is emerging as a precise tool for modifying gene expression without altering DNA sequences. Scientists are developing advanced epigenetic editing systems that can selectively activate or silence genes, paving the way for potential cures for genetic disorders. This innovation is set to reshape the epigenetics market, driving novel treatment strategies for complex diseases.

**4. Integration with Multi-Omics Approaches**

Epigenomics is increasingly being combined with genomics, transcriptomics, and proteomics to gain deeper insights into disease mechanisms. Integrating multiple layers of biological data allows researchers to develop comprehensive models of gene regulation and disease progression. The epigenetics market is benefiting from this holistic approach, enhancing diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic strategies.

**5. Increasing Role in Aging & Longevity Research**

Epigenetic modifications are closely linked to the aging process, influencing age-related diseases and longevity. Researchers are exploring ways to reverse epigenetic changes associated with aging, opening potential avenues for lifespan extension and improved health in later years. The growing focus on age-related epigenetics is fueling advancements in anti-aging therapies and regenerative medicine.

**6. Growing Demand for Epigenetic Biomarkers**

Epigenetic biomarkers are becoming essential tools for diagnostics, disease monitoring, and drug response prediction. These biomarkers help detect cancer at early stages, monitor treatment effectiveness, and identify individuals at risk for various conditions. As the epigenetics market evolves, the demand for biomarker-driven diagnostics is expected to surge, improving personalized treatment approaches.

**7. AI & Big Data Integration**

Artificial intelligence and big data analytics are revolutionizing epigenetic research by enabling faster and more accurate interpretation of complex datasets. Machine learning algorithms can identify epigenetic patterns linked to diseases, accelerating biomarker discovery and drug development. The incorporation of AI is streamlining research in the epigenetics market, making data-driven innovations more accessible.

In September 2024, Ginkgo Bioworks unveiled Ginkgo Datapoints, an AI-driven platform designed to generate large-scale, high-quality biological datasets. This initiative aims to enhance the training of AI models in genomics and healthcare, thereby accelerating biomarker discovery and drug development.

**8. Rising Interest in Environmental Epigenetics**

Scientists are increasingly studying how environmental factors such as pollution, diet, and lifestyle choices impact gene expression. Epigenetic modifications caused by external influences can contribute to chronic diseases, metabolic disorders, and even mental health conditions. The expanding field of environmental epigenetics is shedding light on how lifestyle changes can positively influence genetic health.

**9. Expansion of Epigenetics in Agriculture**

Beyond healthcare, epigenetic research is making significant contributions to agriculture. Scientists are utilizing epigenetic modifications to enhance crop resilience, improve livestock health, and increase agricultural productivity. This expansion is adding a new dimension to the epigenetics market, demonstrating its impact beyond human medicine.

In March 2023, Sound Agriculture launched the Summer Swell tomato, a new slicing variety developed using their On-Demand Breeding process. This non-GMO approach leverages epigenetic techniques to develop plant traits up to ten times faster than traditional methods, resulting in a tomato with superior taste and extended peak flavour.

**10. Surge in Investments & Collaborations**

The rising interest in epigenetic therapies has led to increased collaborations between pharmaceutical companies, biotech firms, and research institutions. Governments and private investors are funding large-scale epigenetic studies, driving the development of new drugs and diagnostic tools. As investment flows into the epigenetics market, the pace of innovation is expected to accelerate, leading to groundbreaking discoveries in the coming years.

In January 2025, [Tune Therapeutics completed](https://tunetx.com/tune-therapeutics-completes-over-175m-in-series-b-financing-to-advance-field-leading-epigenome-editing-programs/) a USD 175 million fundraising round, led by New Enterprise Associates, Hevolution Foundation, and Yosemite, Regeneron Ventures. This funding will accelerate the development of Tune's epigenome editing platform and its therapeutic programs Tune-401, an epigenetic silencer that targets chronic Hepatitis B (HBV).

### **Final Thoughts**

The epigenetics market is evolving rapidly, driven by advancements in personalized medicine, AI-powered research, and multi-omics integration. As scientists continue to unravel the complexities of epigenetic modifications, the potential applications in healthcare, agriculture, and environmental science will continue to expand. From groundbreaking drug discoveries to revolutionary anti-aging interventions, epigenetics is reshaping the future of medicine. The trends highlighted in this blog demonstrate how this field is set to redefine healthcare, offering more precise and effective treatments for various diseases. The future of the epigenetics market holds immense promise, with continuous innovation driving its growth on a global scale.

FULL CONTENT:
![BCC Research Blog](https://blog.bccresearch.com/hs-fs/hubfs/bcc_blog-2022/blogs/BCC_Research_Blog.png?width=188&height=45&name=BCC_Research_Blog.png "BCC Research Blog")
![How Epigenetics Is Powering the Next Wave of Medical Innovation](https://blog.bccresearch.com/hubfs/Cell%20and%20Gene%20Therapy%20Tools%20and%20Reagents%20Market.jpg "How Epigenetics Is Powering the Next Wave of Medical Innovation")

# How Epigenetics Is Powering the Next Wave of Medical Innovation

![](https://blog.bccresearch.com/hubfs/Picture1.jpg)
![date](https://blog.bccresearch.com/hubfs/bcc_blog-2022/blogs/date.svg)

Apr 1, 2025

[Blog](https://blog.bccresearch.com/)

[Life Sciences](https://blog.bccresearch.com/topic/life-sciences)

How Epigenetics Is Powering the Next Wave of Medical Innovation

In recent years, *epigenetics* has emerged as one of the most exciting areas of modern science. But what exactly is epigenetics, and why is it becoming a game-changer in healthcare, agriculture, and biotechnology?

Let’s examine the technologies shaping this space and the booming global market behind it.

## **What is Epigenetics?**

Epigenetics is the study of changes in gene activity that don’t involve changes to the DNA sequence itself. In simpler terms, it’s like software running on your genetic hardware. While your DNA provides the instructions, epigenetics decides *how*, *when*, and *where* those instructions are read and carried out.

Several factors, such as environment, lifestyle, age, and disease, can influence these changes. Epigenetics' potential reversibility makes it powerful, which opens doors to targeted therapies, especially in cancer, neurological disorders, and autoimmune diseases.

## **Why Epigenetics Matters**

The biggest reason epigenetics is so important is because it helps explain how genes are turned “on” or “off.” For example, two people might have the same gene linked to a disease, but only one gets sick. Epigenetic markers—like DNA methylation or histone modification—could be why.

Understanding these markers allows researchers to develop more accurate diagnostic tools and design treatments to switch off harmful genes or reactivate protective ones. This is a major leap toward *personalized medicine*—treatments designed for each person based on their unique genetic and epigenetic makeup.

## **The Global Market Outlook**

With increasing investments in drug development, cancer research, and precision medicine, this market is expected to reach new heights in the coming years.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent advancements such as Perturb-ATAC and novel sequencing techniques enhance the precision of epigenetic research, enabling deeper insights into gene regulation and cellular processes.
Discoveries linking epigenetic modifications to diseases like cancer reveal critical pathways, offering new perspectives on disease progression and potential intervention targets.
Emerging epigenetic therapies, including CRISPR-based editing and targeted drugs, hold promise for personalized medicine, aiming to modify gene expression without altering the DNA sequence.
